item.title: Some damn good lines.
item.content:encoded:
This SCP plays with the "Choose-Your-Own-Adventure" (CYOA) concept incredibly well. Especially the twist at the end to explain what's going on and what the SCP is.

+ (SPOILER WARNING) My favorite quote, which pokes at the CYOA story format brilliantly.
- This is just genius.

"You can't say what you're thinking, you can't even explicitly think it. That would be to contradict a potential past."

"You can't even explain why something happens in this CYOA, because what happens is inevitable, but the reasons behind why/how it happens are controlled by the Reader's choices. All paths converge to the same ending, but the ending isn't caused by one single path/choice; there is no canon, no single explanation for an event that multiple paths lead to."

I love this line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not too sure how I feel about this one… on one hand, the CYOA format was completely unexpected, and I like how its actually part of the containment procedures. On the other, why did the Senior Researcher have to sacrifice himself? Couldn't he just write the story around a random D-class, and then insert said D-class into the story?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: masterpiece, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm floored, honestly floored. A damn good read from start to finish and all the way to each of the cute ways you can screw up. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice use of Inside! Makes you wonder how many other things could be contained with it.

item.title: Re: Good job
item.content:encoded: Is that an invitation to use this format?

item.title: Re: A few questions
item.content:encoded:
1. Nope. Dr Greaves makes an appearance in SCP-3211, again as an amnestics chemist, but other than that he only exists in SCP-3939's containment story. Maybe they're the same person. Who knows.

2. It was as a favour to a reviewer.

3. Nope. It's just the tip of the iceberg for some vast expanse of emotional backstory, except the rest of the iceberg doesn't actually exist.

4. I'm not sure what exactly you mean by this. All of the branches are fictional, and in all branches, Carlos is absent in some way. The only time the branches ever interact with each other is when they converge at the end.

item.title: A few questions
item.content:encoded:
So, I really like this SCP. I have a few narrative questions on it.
First, is the abnormally powerful amnestics and Dr. Greaves a reference to anything?
Second, why did you choose that specific D-class designation? That SCP seems to have no relation to this SCP.
Third, is the mentioned incident with Crickett a reference to anything?
And finally, if I get this correctly, since in that branch Carlos leaves the assignment in one way or another, in the other branches he's absent. How exactly do the various branches interact, or is the story purely fictional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This hurts my brain but, like, in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Go cast your fancy magic someplace else."

Darn computer wizards. :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If someone could draw up images for this skip, this would be a very cool visual novel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, that makes sense! I didn't notice that little detail, but yeah, I don't think you'd be able to have the same response like that over and over either. Thanks for responding!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yours is a lot cleaner than mine (warning: 13 MB of terrible photo quality)

Makes perfect sense to me (after a little bit of revision of both diagrams), but might not make much sense to anyone else :P

I remember that having the second "loop" feed into the first was a very deliberate decision. The only problem is, I can't remember why!

+ I just took a good, hard look at those offsets and both of our diagrams...
– hide block

I just took a good, hard look at those offsets and both of our diagrams… and I legitimately can't work out what the difference between those two sections is. When writing this I had to balance continuity between the prose and the article itself, and I was thinking that maybe I had to make this weird loop-shift to keep the articles consistent, but the articles in both sections are identical. And if there is a difference between the two loops, surely it's breaking continuity to be switching from one to the other?

I took a closer look, and worked out that the following pairs of articles from both loops should be pretty much identical:

32 = 36
12 = 14
64 = 8
51 = 2
(your diagram: top loop is on the left, bottom loop is on the right)

32 and 36 are exactly the same. The only difference is that in 32, you've already seen Sally today because you decided to get her to write some of the article. You then proceeded to not believe what she said about 3939, so you performed a quick analysis yourself and then invited her along again for a more in-depth analysis.
In 36, both you and Sally are going down there for the first time.

The same difference is amplified between 12 and 14. In 14, you and Sally are experiencing 3939's anomalous rotation for the first time, together. In 12, Sally is already aware of the rotation, and you feel guilty for not believing her when she told you about it.

Then, when you touch the vinyl record itself, both 12 and 14 point to 8.

Both 64 and 8 are 100% identical, but now I can see (and/or I've remembered) that there's a contextual difference between them.

It wouldn't make sense to loop between 12 and 64 forever. 12 contains the emotional response from the guilt of ignoring Sally's report. Once you've had that reaction once, it doesn't make sense to be able to have it over and over again. As a result, both loops point to the much more emotionally-neutral 8 instead of the guilty 64.

So, the reason why the loops are weird is because offset 12 contains an emotional reaction which logically can't be experienced twice. In order to prevent it from being possible to see offset 12 twice in the same read-through, I broke the loop.

I hope that clears things up a bit for the both of us :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If nothing else, this skip wins a prize for having 67 freaking offsets. (Do any other SCPs have that many offsets? None that I'm aware of, that's for certain.) This skip reminds me a bit of SCP-910 in that it contains a text adventure game related to the skip, albeit SCP-910 utilizes Twine instead of offsets to do this, and the skip itself doesn't change based on the choices made. Took me hecka long to explore all the options in this skip, but I adored every moment of it. Also, unlike SCP-910's text adventure, the branching paths of the narrative are crucial to the nature of the skip.

Since I'm a massive nerd, I made a map of the offsets of this skip 'cause I'm terrible.

Click for nerd stuff!
Eww, too nerdy, make it go away!

The text adventure contains 2 loops. When navigating to offset 66, you can loop between offsets 6 and 53. This loop is for repeatedly giving the two stupid answers to why the skip wasn't already classified. When navigating to offsets 36 and 12, you can loop between offsets 8 and 14. This loop is for repeatedly walking around the gramophone and touching the record, ad infinitum. Despite there being two separate parts in this diagram where you can do these actions, only one of them loops, due to the second feeding into the first on offset 12 for some reason ("terminating" the potential loop for the second by feeding it into the loop in the first). I'm not sure if this was intentional or not, since offset 12 SHOULD link to offset 64 for touching the record, not offset 8 (since 64 was linked to in the offset prior to offset 12).

Hope what I just said made literally any sense.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hidden branches, amazing idea, choose how you do the file, bit of a tale style experience of how the SCP works.

Best SCP I've ever seen. Ever.

+1.

item.title: Im literally researcher ■■■■ ■■■■■■■■
item.content:encoded:
Lmao my name has the same number of letters. This added to the awesomeness to the article and my love for the foundation made the perfect immersive scp experience.
I am very reluctant to meta articles but this is on some other level. Thank you mr. Croquem, thanks for the wild ride.

item.title: Magnificent.
item.content:encoded: Absolutely magnificent. There's really nothing more I can say about this one. *applauds*

item.title: So there isn't another end?
item.content:encoded:
Really, really many pages.
It takes me nearly two hours to check every link and finally found out nothing.
I don't want to try again.

item.title: THIS is how meta should be done
item.content:encoded:
Format screw that is worth it. Engaging story and a clear grasp of how the foundation works. Terror elements, but also humour and world-building.

This is one of my new favourite SCPs, and if more "meta" SCPs were like this, I would not be so against Series III and Series IV over-reliance on meta components.

This is a great SCP and I praise the author

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But… I don't wanna die.
+1, I love format screw SCPs. This is one of the best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you be a little more specific?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Offset 7 leads to Offset 15, and that causes there to be a continuity error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dittoing everything here, but staying at a novote.

item.title: Re: Author Post - [NUMBER RESERVED; AWAITING RESEARCHER]
item.content:encoded: The amount of work and dedication you've put into that is commendable. Thank you very much! I hope it wasn't a complete chore for you :P

item.title: Re: Author Post - [NUMBER RESERVED; AWAITING RESEARCHER]
item.content:encoded:
Finally I finished translating this article…It took me almost 12 hours to translate and post it on our wiki.

The article is amazing, and it brings a brand new type of writing mode to the Foundation.

However, I think I have to say: Poor Carlos！！What a pity！！！！Why you treat him in this way！！！

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awesome premise to this one. I've seen some CYOA formatting before, but this one takes the cake for the best one I've seen yet. Amazing story, leading to how the document is published and how the
Senior Researcher discovers the anomalous property of 3939. Stunning in all aspects that interest me. Well done. +1 I'm proud of. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe it could be because the one above this one in the "March 2018 Top Rated New Pages" 3393, is a few weeks older than this one. At least that's my idea, someone else probably has a better one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why doesn't this article have more upvotes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good and I love memes. The ones that kill "you", not your "social life".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Happy to clarify.

+ Wrapped in a collapsible for the sake of spoilers
– hide block

SCP-3939 has no physical form, but exists as an unknown hostile entity embedded into an extremely aggressive meme.

This is the only line that describes what 3939 "is". The rest of the document describe what it does.

To reword it, 3939 is an idea or a concept. A few examples of things like this are the concept of love and the concept of time passing. In this case, SCP-3939 is literally the concept of itself. It is self-recursive and intentionally difficult to both understand and imagine.

Like a driver in a car, there's someone - or something - controlling SCP-3939, some sentient intelligent entity. Without this entity, SCP-3939 is just the idea of itself and doesn't matter at all. With this entity - the driver - SCP-3939 can jump around and move from object to object.

By this I mean that SCP-3939 redefines itself. Let's say that the concept of SCP-3939 describes the content of a book. When someone reads the book, SCP-3939 has the ability to then redefine itself as that person (or more accurately, as that person's memory of the text in the book). It is then able to physically control the person, which is what makes it dangerous, detectable, and worthy of containment.

When the person being controlled by SCP-3939 observes and thinks about some information - for example any text, or a conversation - it is then able to redefine itself as that information. In the case of text, this means that it can lie in wait for someone to read it and then redefine itself as that person. In the case of a conversation, it can just immediately redefine itself as the other person who was having the conversation.

Because the entity is sentient, it is motivated to redefine itself as other people and not just hide in data, because that's really boring.

In this explanation, I've used "redefine as" as opposed to "infect".

It's all the redefining that makes SCP-3939 so confusing. Ultimately, the SCP itself is secondary to the CYOA, which should comprise the bulk of any enjoyment you derive from this. So long as you vaguely understand what 3939 is, and why that justifies the CYOA, then we're good.

If you have any more questions, let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am withholding my vote for now because something isn't clear to me yet.

The cyoa story is well done, as everyone is saying, and the format screw is accomplished immaculately. I have no beef with that. The story is even interesting to me.

But when it comes to the final skip, it seems totally undefined. Or at least underdescribed. All of that stuff about how easily it infects people and media and yet I still have no idea what it is or does.

Maybe I'm just stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what a cool looking website tbh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was just excellent, perfect execution of an ambitious idea.

item.title: (no title)

item.title: Re: Good job
item.content:encoded: I think I'd prefer to see what you can come up with!

item.title: Good job
item.content:encoded: I very much enjoyed this one and this is probably my favorite one so far can you make more like this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sleep —> Look at the picture —> Say it's perfect

item.title: I approve
item.content:encoded:
This article is simply top notch.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a question, how do you get to the one where the boss strangles you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shouldn't it be the Foundation Star, and not Crest?

Also agreed with Flyndragon, i kinda wanted a narrative choose-your-own-adventure skip that doesn't use the narrative as the containment procedures. I upvoted this anyway simply because I loved this idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't really say anything that hasn't been said. +1

I can point out that the Doctor we "Play" as has enough black boxes for his name to be "Your Namehere." I'm not sure if that was intentional or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice! I don't think it's the most common piece of knowledge. I just remember what happened when my buddy put a vinyl record on an old gramophone. The tracking force literally causes the vinyl to scrape up in strips behind the needle.

Still though minor nitpick in a great meta scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's better than many games…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was actually about to downvote when I scrolled down. Then I found the story.
+1, for catching me off guard like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The premise of the article itself is absolutely beautiful and I love this original style. You easily earned my (as well as others') upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The justification for that in-universe was fine (though I admit the self-awareness of it made me kind of sour on it), but I just think I'd prefer my adventures to have some kind of meaning to it, no matter how minor. I'm not a fan of games where you're given choices that don't matter anyway (to reference a game brought up in this thread). You're a solid writer though. Godspeed on your future endeavors, no matter how ambitious or simple they are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Thank you for this comment.
– hide block

I actually agree. I'd've loved to write a story where you go in, you run different tests on it, send in D-Class to do different things… the different D-Class members would each have different personalities and affect the outcome of the experiment in different ways. And by the end you'd have a bunch of different documents, maybe generated by javascript, each detailing a unique SCP-3939 that no one else would see.

However, I started with the final idea and worked backwards. It was more important to me to explain the final document (and justify why the CYOA exists in the first place) than it was for the CYOA to explain the final document. That's what I was going for, and that's what this is.

I'd like to see a well-written, truly fleshed out multiple choice story in the future. But to quote SCP-3939:

You see, fundamentally, this just isn't a good multiple choice story.

That was the initial idea, and that's what I stuck to.

I very much appreciate your criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for your input!

Looks like you're right - the record should be shellac. The record in the article is made of vinyl, even though it shouldn't be. I've acknowledged this in Scene 24: "Take a look at the spinning vinyl record."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who adores and voraciously consumes CYOAs, I'm actually going to voice the most lukewarm opinion on this, and say that I don't think it was quite as good as people are making it out to be. I think the fact that it effectively ends the same no matter what (and that the final offset for every option then turns out to be totally the same despite multiple different paths) is somewhat of a cop-out, and I might as well just be clicking random buttons. The writing in all the different options were good, but you stalled out on wrapping it up.

That said, I upvoted because the follow-through on it as well as the oddly unique form of justification for this in-universe (it doesn't need a justification IMO, but the fact that you did it makes it really interesting) is pretty damn good. I just want people writing CYOAs for this site in the future to not end them the way it was done here. That was a serious disappointment for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hot damn. I honestly don't know what else to say other than this is really fucking good. Prime example of a format screw done right (good reason, keeps the reader invested). Even without the format screw, I think the idea of the skip is solid as well, the CYOA aspect just adds to it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved it.

Have one slight annoyance though. Gramophones use shellac records not vinyl ones. Your image source even has that in the name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I, for some silly, potentially anomalous reason were attracted by well-written and interesting pages, I would A. probably limit my activity on this site and, more importantly B. want to marry this page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the sort of thing that keeps me coming back to the Foundation. Well thought out, constructed, written. Now I want to go back and read the other branches, even though I know the paths of story lead but to the grave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… I'm speechless. This is a phenomenal amount of work, and it's all good. Normally I say that the amount of work put into something doesn't obligate an upvote, but…

This is a solid fucking skip. The format screw is, most importantly, justified well, as well as executed well. I tried every path before voting, and they're all wonderfully written.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 instantly, lord I LOVE meta stuff like this, especially when it's this well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I was highly skeptical of the format screw at first but I upvoted during the compelling read. Then, the fact that you made the story an inoculation against the information contained within the properly formatted SCP article was just genius.

I also enjoyed a lot of the "hidden branches."

+ show block
– hide block

Like being able to amnestitize Carlos into a D-Class personnel, or having your boss attack you at the throat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just Monika, the SCP I probably would have upvoted anyway, but I am very glad the format screw was a part of the containment procedures. This is really cleverly designed and like others have said, must have been a ton of work. It all paid off!

And after having read, as far as I can tell, the whole thing, I have to say Carlos is the best part of this, and I regret the "click the first one" read-through completely omits him. Can't fit a whole ass in an hour! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can’t express how much I love this article. Made it to my new top 3 list.
